caption: Stated 2010 goal: a great Internet movie service combining DVD-by-mail with streaming, growing subscribers and EPS every year — the pre-pure-streaming strategy NFLX has since fully pivoted from.
DVD-era subscriber growth: 7.5M (4Q07) to 12.3M (4Q09) — a subscriber count NFLX no longer discloses, anchoring the base before the streaming era.
Monthly churn 3.9%-4.6% across 2007-2009, ending 3.9% — a retention KPI NFLX dropped over a decade ago; the only quantified churn baseline in the archive.
Subscriber Acquisition Cost falling from $34.58 (4Q07) to $25.23 (4Q09) — a marketing-efficiency metric long since discontinued.
FCF build, 4Q09: OCF $105.8M less $22.4M capex and $57.0M content-library spend = $30.2M FCF — content already ~half of operating cash even in the DVD era.
Summary financials 2004-2009: revenue $501M to $1,670M, operating profit $19M to $192M, EPS $0.33 to $1.98 — the pre-streaming financial base.
WBD deal strategic rationale: Netflix's 190-country reach plus Warner Bros.' library, franchises and HBO Max. Deal was signed Dec 2025 and TERMINATED Feb 27 2026.
WBD transaction summary: $27.75/share ($23.25 cash + $4.50 NFLX stock, collared), cash on hand plus new debt, expected close 12-18 months — none of which happened (deal died in ~12 weeks).
WBD deal economics: $82.7B EV at 25.2x CY26E EBITDA pre-synergies / 14.3x post-synergies, funded by $50B new acquisition debt — the largest leverage signal in NFLX's deck history.
WBD synergy/accretion targets: at least $2-3B run-rate cost savings by year three, GAAP-EPS accretive by year two, pledge to keep investment-grade ratings via rapid debt paydown.
Netflix committed to MAINTAIN Warner Bros.' theatrical film releases and HBO Max & HBO — a reversal of Netflix's long-standing direct-to-streaming, no-theatrical doctrine.

Business-acceleration thesis: management argued WBD's IP/library would attract and retain subscribers, drive engagement and incremental operating income 'for decades' — the case for the now-dead deal.

Nielsen US TV viewshare, Oct 2025: YouTube 12.9%, Disney 11.4%, NBCUniversal 8.6%, FOX 8.4%, Paramount 8.2%, Netflix 8.0% — the only third-party share-of-viewing datapoint in the archive; Netflix trails YouTube and Disney in total US TV time.
